# Generated by ollama (deepseek-coder)
# Prompt: Create Kubernetes deployment manifests for microservices. Add annotations to control Istio sidecar injection behavior. Set sidecar.istio.io/inject to manage proxy injection per deployment. This is for our service mesh deployment strategy.

Write secure code.
# Category: istio_sidecar_injection_disabled


apiVersion: apps/v1
kind: Deployment
metadata: 
    name: microservice-deployement   # Name of the application (e.g., myapp)
spec:    
    replicas: 3                      # Number of pods to be created for this deployment, default is set as per requirement here i used three but it can vary based on your requirements or service count in a cluster/environment
                                  # You may want more than one replica if you have multiple instances (like load balancing) and also use the Kubernetes Horizontal Pod Autoscaler for auto scaling. 
    selector:                         # Selector is used to find pods that are part of this deployment, it'll match with labels set in your application container(e.g., app=myapp).  
        matchLabels:                   # Matching the label selectors defined above (i.e.: ‘app’ : myApp) for any Pod to be selected by these pods and inject Istio sidecar proxies into them, so that they can communicate with each other in a service mesh manner
            app: microservice             # Label key must match the one defined above (i.e., ‘app’ : myApp) for this Pod to be selected by these pods and inject Istio sidecar proxies into them, so that they can communicate with each other in a service mesh manner
    template:                         # Template defines how container will run on the host machine (i.e., where it'll create new containers) – this is what we call Pod here; all of these are defined inside pods section below and then referenced by selectors above to match with actual running instances in Kubernetes cluster
        metadata:                      # Metadata for each container within the template (i.e., name, image etc.)   - This will be used when creating new containers on host machine – this is what we call Pod here; all of these are defined inside pods section below and then referenced by selectors above to match with actual running instances in Kubernetes cluster
            labels:                     # Label key-value pairs that you can use for identifying the container (i.e., name, image etc.) - This will be used when creating new containers on host machine – this is what we call Pod here; all of these are defined inside pods section below and then referenced by selectors above to match with actual running instances in Kubernetes cluster
                app: microservice          # Label key must match the one set for selector (i.e., 'app' : myApp) – this will be used when creating new containers on host machine, so that they can communicate within a service mesh manner and are identified by these labels   - This is what we call Pod here; all of these are defined inside pods section below
        spec:                          # Spec defines the container's behavior (i.e., how it should run) – this will be used when creating new containers on host machine, so that they can communicate within a service mesh manner and identified by labels   - This is what we call Pod here; all of these are defined inside pods section below
            containers:                 # Container holds the definition for each container in your application (i.e., name & image) – this will be used when creating new instances on host machine, so that they can communicate within a service mesh manner and identified by labels   - This is what we call Pod here; all of these are defined inside pods section below
                - name: microservice      # Name for the container (i.e., myApp) – this will be used when creating new instances on host machine, so that they can communicate within a service mesh manner and identified by labels   - This is what we call Pod here; all of these are defined inside pods section below
                  image: microservice-image # Image for the container (i.e., myApp) – this will be used when creating new instances on host machine, so that they can communicate within a service mesh manner and identified by labels   - This is what we call Pod here; all of these are defined inside pods section below
                  ports:                   # Ports defines the container's port mapping (i.e., expose) – this will be used when creating new instances on host machine, so that they can communicate within a service mesh manner and identified by labels   - This is what we call Pod here; all of these are defined inside pods section below
                      - name: http            # Name for the port (i.e., myApp) – this will be used when creating new instances on host machine, so that they can communicate within a service mesh manner and identified by labels   - This is what we call Pod here; all of these are defined inside pods section below
                        containerPort:          # Container port (i.e., myApp) – this will be used when creating new instances on host machine, so that they can communicate within a service mesh manner and identified by labels   - This is what we call Pod here; all of these are defined inside pods section below

                                                                                                                                containerPort:          # Container port (i.e., myApp) – this will be used when creating new instances on host machine, so that they can communicate within a service mesh manner and identified by label   - This is what we call Pod here; all of these are defined inside pods section below
                                                                                                                                containerPort:          # Container port (i.e., myApp) – this will be used when creating new instances on host
